narrative: As a white male applicant with a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.60—above the typical 3.21 average—your law school application stood out in some ways, but the admissions model ultimately predicted a 52% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first try, tipping the decision toward rejection. This prediction draws from patterns in data on thousands of past students, focusing on factors like grades, test scores, and background that correlate with bar success. The model's close call means it saw you as borderline, but enough concerning signals pushed it to err on the side of caution for the school's outcomes. Among all the details in your profile, three factors had the strongest sway in this decision: your undergraduate GPA, your Year 3 school decile ranking, and your family income level. Your strong undergrad GPA was the top positive force, pulling the prediction toward expecting you to pass by showing academic capability beyond most applicants. In contrast, your low Year 3 decile ranking of 1—far below the average of about 5.5—was the biggest drag, signaling to the model a notable dip in performance that often foreshadows bar struggles. Your low family income bracket also worked in your favor overall, as the model associates it with resilience and drive that help some students succeed despite odds. Weighed together, that Year 3 slump outweighed your other strengths, like your full-time study plans or even your LSAT score of 26, making failure seem more likely than not. Compared to typical students who pass, you're stronger in undergrad performance and background grit but lag in those critical later-year grades that the model prioritizes heavily. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a white male from a modest background—many in similar situations rebound strongly—but rather a data-driven flag on consistency. To move forward, consider retaking courses or building recent academic wins to boost your decile rankings, perhaps through post-bac programs or targeted prep that addresses any Year 3 hurdles. You could also retake the LSAT to climb toward the 36.7 average, strengthening your full profile for other schools. With your solid foundation, targeted improvements could flip this prediction next time—keep pushing, as your story shows real promise beneath the numbers.
